total number of active nodes across all 𝐿 layers at termination. The 
specific intra-layer and inter-layer activation probabilities are formally 
defined in Section 4.1.
4 . The proposed algorithm
4.1 . Multilayer heterogeneous network independent cascade model
Information diffusion in multilayer networks exhibits greater com­
plexity than spreading dynamics in monolayer networks [ 15]. This 
intricacy stems from the heterogeneous topological interconnection 
structures across different platform layers. Users who are active in one 
layer may engage in cross-platform information exchange, which may 
set off information cascades across multiple social networks. Therefore, 
the interconnections between layers serve as a vital determinant in the 
diffusion process. The likelihood of inter-layer diffusion is correlated 
with the structural connectivity patterns between layers [ 49]. Densely 
interconnected layer pairs are likely to facilitate more frequent cross-
platform information transfer, while sparsely connected layers exhibit 
weaker propagation tendencies. When implementing the IC model on 
each layer in isolation, it oversimplifies the diffusion processes in mul­
tilayer networks and fails to capture the intricate nature of inter-layer 
interactions [13]. Therefore, it is crucial to combine monolayer spread­
ing dynamics with topology-aware inter-layer diffusion mechanisms to 
model the diffusion process.

Fig. 2. An example of the MHIC model.
Given a multilayer heterogeneous network G, the multilayer hetero­
geneous network independent cascade model is proposed to capture 
topology-driven cross-layer information propagation. The MHIC model 
considers both intra-layer and inter-layer propagation mechanisms. In 
intra-layer propagation, the IC model is adopted, where a node acti­
vated at time step 𝑡 attempts to activate each of its unactivated neighbors 
within the same layer with probability 𝑝, and only successfully activated 
neighbors will participate in subsequent propagation at time step 𝑡+ 1 . 
In inter-layer propagation, once a node 𝑢 is activated at time step 𝑡 in 
layer 𝑖, it attempts to activate its connected nodes in other layers 𝑗 (𝑗≠ 𝑖 ) 
through existing inter-layer edges (𝑢, 𝑣) ∈𝐸 𝑖𝑗 with probability 𝑞𝑖𝑗 at time 
step 𝑡+ 1 . The diffusion process terminates when no new nodes are ac­
tivated in any layer, and the total number of activated nodes 𝜎(S) is 
then computed. The inter-layer propagation probability 𝑞𝑖𝑗 is defined in 
Eq. (2).

where | 𝐸𝑖𝑗 |  denotes the number of inter-layer edges connecting layer 
𝑖 and layer 𝑗, and | 𝑉𝑖|  and | 𝑉𝑗 |  represent the number of nodes in each 
respective layer. The inter-layer connectivity density 𝑞𝑖𝑗 quantifies the 
likelihood of cross-platform information spread and captures the het­
erogeneity of inter-layer coupling strengths, as real-world multilayer 
systems often exhibit vastly different connection densities across layer 
pairs. This density-based formulation is consistent with theoretical find­
ings on spreading processes in multilayer networks [49], which identify 
macro-level inter-layer coupling strength as the primary driver of cross-
layer cascade dynamics. Notably, 𝑞𝑖𝑗 varies across different layer pairs, 
directly reflecting the structural coupling strengths between distinct 
platform pairs. 
Fig. 2 illustrates the information diffusion process under the MHIC 
model. At the initial time step 𝑡= 0 , seed nodes {4,7}  in layer 1 (gray) 
and seed nodes {𝑐, 𝑑} in layer 2 (pink) are activated. The dashed lines 
between the two layers represent inter-layer connections, which deter­
mine the inter-layer propagation probability 𝑞12.

The dashed lines 
between the two layers represent inter-layer connections, which deter­
mine the inter-layer propagation probability 𝑞12. At time step 𝑡= 1 , the 
intra-layer propagation occurs: within layer 1, node 4 successfully acti­
vates node 6, node 7 successfully activates nodes 3 and 5, while neither 
node 4 nor node 7 activates node 2. Simultaneously, in layer 2, node 𝑐
activates node 𝑒, and node 𝑑 activates node 𝑔. The cross-layer propaga­
tion occurs: specifically, node 4 in layer 1 successfully activates node 𝑎 in 
layer 2 via cross-layer propagation, while node 𝑑 in layer 2 propagates its 
influence to node 2 in layer 1. All other cross-layer activation attempts 
fail. At time step 𝑡= 2 , node 2 fails to activate node 1, while node 𝑔 ac­
tivates node 𝑓. Regarding cross-layer propagation, node 6 successfully 
activates node 𝑏 in the other layer. This process demonstrates how the 
MHIC model captures the interaction between intra-layer propagation 
and topology-driven cross-layer cascading effects.
4.2 . SACRL for the MHIM problem
To address the influence maximization problem in multilayer het­
erogeneous networks under the MHIC model, we introduce the SACRL 
algorithm that combines structure-aware node representation learn­
ing with deep reinforcement learning to identify layer-specific optimal 
seed sets. SACRL establishes a specialized structure-aware representa­
tion learning framework integrated with deep reinforcement learning. 
First, a multilayer heterogeneous network embedding module is de­
signed. Rather than utilizing conventional attention mechanisms, this 
module features a graph transformer encoder augmented with a cross-
layer message passing mechanism to process the dense, many-to-many 
inter-layer connection topologies. Additionally, it incorporates specific 
feature augmentations to ensure the resulting representations accurately 
capture both intra-layer topological importance and asymmetric inter-
layer propagation potential.

__data__.text:
Additionally, it incorporates specific 
feature augmentations to ensure the resulting representations accurately 
capture both intra-layer topological importance and asymmetric inter-
layer propagation potential. Second, a Dueling DQN module is employed 
to decompose the action-value function into separate state-value and 
action-advantage streams. This module utilizes an expected influence es­
timator as a proxy reward. SACRL identifies optimal layer-specific seed 
sets and maximizes the global influence spread across the multilayer 
heterogeneous network. The overall framework is illustrated in Fig. 3. 
4.2.1 . Multilayer heterogeneous network embedding module
For the IM task in multilayer heterogeneous networks, node embed­
dings must accurately reflect the structural importance and potential 
influence of nodes across multiple layers. To this end, a multilayer 
heterogeneous network embedding module is designed that integrates 
handcrafted structural features with a Graph Transformer encoder to 
generate node representations.
To capture topological properties, a five-dimensional structural fea­
ture vector 𝐟𝑣 ∈R 5 is computed for each node 𝑣 in the multilayer 
heterogeneous network. Five complementary centrality measures are 
selected to characterize node importance from distinct structural per­
spectives. Degree centrality quantifies direct connectivity as an indicator 
of local influence capacity. Average neighbor degree reflects the propen­
sity of a node to connect to well-connected neighbors, distinguishing 
nodes attached to influential hubs from those in peripheral regions. 
Eigenvector centrality captures recursive importance by accounting 
for the centrality of neighbors. PageRank models influence through a 
random-walk perspective. Finally, the K-shell index reveals the hierar­
chical core-periphery structure, identifying nodes in densely connected 
cores.
Formally, for a node 𝑣∈ 𝑉 𝑖 in layer 𝐺𝑖 = ( 𝑉𝑖, 𝐸𝑖) with intra-
layer neighbor set N𝑖(𝑣), the local features based on the immediate 
neighborhood are defined by Eq.

The two features are dynamically 
updated at each decision step, enabling the agent to perceive the current 
coverage status and avoid redundant selections.
Finally, each node is represented by a 70-dimensional feature vector 
𝐱𝑣 formed by concatenating three components: five structural metrics, 
two binary indicators, and a 63-dimensional vector of ones. The struc­
tural metrics capture complementary aspects of topological importance. 
The two binary indicators are dynamically updated during decision-
making to provide current state information. To alleviate the limitation 
of sparse 7-dimensional features, the input is augmented with a 63-
dimensional constant vector. After the first linear projection, this vector 
acts as a learnable bias with 63 independent parameters, richer than a 
scalar bias. This shifts the ELU pre-activations of structurally peripheral 
nodes, whose centrality-based features are near zero, into the effective 
gradient regime, preventing low-gradient saturation during training. 
The feature design offers a sufficiently expressive input space for the 
graph transformer encoder to learn structural representations of nodes.
Given the initial node feature matrix 𝐗∈ R | 𝑉| ×𝑑 where 𝑑= 70 , a 
Graph Transformer encoder is employed to learn the node representa­
tions. The encoder operates on the multilayer heterogeneous network 
topology with edge set 𝐸, enabling information propagation across lay­
ers. For node 𝑣 at the 𝓁-th encoding level, the embedding 𝐡(𝓁)

while the final level outputs embeddings in R𝑑ℎ. In each attention head

4.2.2 . Dueling DQN module
The Dueling DQN module constitutes the decision-making core of 
the proposed algorithm. The dueling network separately estimates state 
value and action advantage functions, then aggregates them to obtain 
the final Q-values for seed selection.
Traditional DQN estimates the action-value function 𝑄(𝑠, 𝑎) directly, 
which prevents it from distinguishing between the intrinsic value of 
a state and the relative advantage gained by executing a specific ac­
tion. The Dueling DQN addresses this limitation by decomposing

4.2.3 . Training with the SACRL
The training of SACRL employs Dueling DQN with prioritized ex­
perience replay and N-step returns. Firstly, the synthetic three-layer 
networks are utilized for training and validation, generated by the Erdös-
Rényi (ER) network and Barabási-Albert (BA) network. The training 
dataset comprises a small-scale network with 50 nodes per layer, while 
the validation dataset is expanded to 500 nodes per layer to evaluate 
the generalization capabilities. The inter-layer edges between different 
layers are generated randomly.
Furthermore, training RL agents for influence maximization involves 
high computational costs for evaluating the influence spread, which 
requires expensive Monte Carlo simulations. Therefore, a novel proxy re­
ward based on an expected influence estimator is designed. The reward 
𝑟𝑡 after selecting node 𝑣 is defined as:
𝑟𝑡 = Φ(𝑆(𝑡+1)
𝑖 ) − Φ(𝑆(𝑡)
𝑖 ) (18)

𝑖  and 𝑆(𝑡+1)
𝑖  denote the seed sets of the current target layer 𝑖
at time steps 𝑡 and 𝑡 + 1, respectively, and Φ(⋅) is the expected influ­
ence estimator for the target layer.

The complete training procedure is summarized in Algorithm 2. For 
each episode, a multilayer heterogeneous network is sampled from the 
training set, and a target layer is randomly selected. This random layer 
selection strategy enables the model to learn a layer-agnostic policy that 
generalizes across different network layers. The agent interacts with the 
environment using the 𝜖-greedy policy, and transitions are processed 
through the N-step buffer before being stored in the prioritized replay 
memory. The model is periodically evaluated on the validation set. To 
ensure the assessment of the learned policy, the evaluation is performed 
using 2000 Monte Carlo simulations. The best-performing model based 
on influence spread is saved.
4.2.4 . Seed selection module
During the training phase, the model updates its parameters to ap­
proximate the optimal decision-making policy. In each episode, the 
target layer is selected via stochastic sampling instead of a determin­
istic fixed sequence. This stochastic approach provides the necessary 
state diversity to prevent the agent from overfitting to specific layer or­
derings, thereby ensuring the learning of a truly layer-agnostic policy 
that generalizes across heterogeneous network structures. The learned 
parameters effectively encode the strategy for identifying the most in­
fluential seeds. In the inference phase, the trained model is deployed 
to identify seed nodes in the real-world multilayer heterogeneous net­
work. The selection is performed layer by layer using a greedy policy.

5 . Experimental results and analysis
5.1 . Experimental settings
The hyperparameter configurations for the implementation of the 
proposed SACRL framework are specified as follows. The Adam opti­
mizer is utilized for SACRL training with a learning rate of 5 × 10−5 and a 
discount factor 𝛾 = 0.99. The prioritized experience replay buffer is con­
figured with a capacity of 20, 000, and the exploration rate 𝜖 is decayed 
linearly from 1.0 to 0.05 over the training epochs. Both the value and 
advantage streams in the Dueling DQN architecture consist of two fully 
connected layers with 128 hidden units each, incorporating LayerNorm 
and ELU activation functions.
5.2 . Datasets and baselines
Six real-world multilayer heterogeneous networks were included to 
illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed SACRL. In the six networks, 
the Arxiv network is a collaboration network where each layer repre­
sents a different subject category in the preprint archive. The Aminer 
network is a multilayer heterogeneous collaboration network consist­
ing of top conferences, whose edges represent author connections.

__data__.text:
In the six networks, 
the Arxiv network is a collaboration network where each layer repre­
sents a different subject category in the preprint archive. The Aminer 
network is a multilayer heterogeneous collaboration network consist­
ing of top conferences, whose edges represent author connections. The 
Paris transportation network is a multilayer network where each layer 
corresponds to a distinct transportation mode, interconnected into an in­
tegrated system. The last three networks are multilayer networks built 
upon intra-university Facebook social data, where each layer corre­
sponds to a distinct academic cohort and captures intra-cohort social 
relationships. The inter-cohort connections are modeled as inter-layer 
edges. 
Table 1 provides the basic information of these datasets.
To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed SACRL, the experiments 
are conducted against the following nine baseline algorithms:
• CIM [41]: CIM applies the maximal clique detection strategy to 
the multilayer network to identify the seed set that maximizes the 
information spread.
• CPR [12]: CPR extends the PageRank algorithm to multilayer 
networks to characterize the importance of nodes through an 
information feedback mechanism.
• CWIIIF [30]: CWIIIF integrates the K-shell and betweenness cen­
trality methods into the multilayer network to identify seed nodes.
• AGNN [50]: AGNN employs the autoencoder and graph neural net­
work architectures on complex networks to identify the vital nodes 
in the network.
• BiGDN [27]: BiGDN combines deep reinforcement learning with 
graph neural networks within an end-to-end model to identify the 
most influential seed nodes in social networks.

__data__.text:
• GEHC [51]: GEHC combines the DeepWalk method with degree 
and K-shell hybrid centrality, and then calculates node influence by 
measuring weighted embedding similarities between neighboring 
nodes.
• DC: Degree centrality computes the degree of each node in 
multilayer networks and selects seed nodes based on the degree 
scores.
• K-shell: K-shell decomposes the network and ranks nodes by 
coreness to identify influential seeds.
5.3 . Ablation experiments
To validate the effectiveness of the structural feature extraction, 
Dueling DQN architecture, and cross-layer message passing mecha­
nisms in the proposed SACRL algorithm, ablation experiments were 
conducted on six multilayer heterogeneous networks. Five ablation vari­
ants were designed: (1) SACRL-NS, which removes the structural feature 
extraction, (2) SACRL-DQN, which replaces the proposed Dueling DQN 
architecture with a standard DQN, (3) SACRL-NC, which eliminates the 
cross-layer message passing mechanism, (4) SACRL-CD, which augments 
the node feature vector with a normalized inter-layer degree feature to 
explicitly encode cross-layer connectivity, and (5) SACRL-CV, which re­
moves the 63-dimensional constant vector from the node feature input. 
In the ablation studies, the intra-layer spread probability was fixed at 
𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑟𝑎 = 0 .01, the seed set sizes were varied between 10 and 50 using a 
step size of 10, and the Monte Carlo simulations were performed 2000 
times. The results of the ablation studies are illustrated in Fig. 4.
As shown in Fig. 4 , SACRL consistently outperforms SACRL-NS, 
SACRL-DQN, and SACRL-NC across all six multilayer heterogeneous net­
works, validating the contribution of each core component. When 𝑘 =
50, the SACRL demonstrates substantial improvements over SACRL-NS.

50, the SACRL demonstrates substantial improvements over SACRL-NS. 
Specifically, the improvement rates are approximately 26.63% on the 
Cal65 network and 32.25% on the Aminer network. By integrating both 
local and global structural features, the SACRL encodes the topological 
importance of nodes within each layer. The multi-perspective features 
can be used to generate higher-quality node embeddings. The perfor­
mance gains are particularly pronounced in Fig. 4 (d), where SACRL 
achieves a 31.46% improvement over the DQN variant at 𝑘 = 50 . By 
decomposing the action-value function into separate state-value and ad­
vantage streams, the dueling architecture enables the network to learn 
the intrinsic value of states and evaluate the relative importance of ac­
tions. This leads to the identification of higher-quality seed sets in the 
multilayer heterogeneous network. Similarly, on the Arxiv network in 
Fig. 4(a), SACRL outperforms SACRL-NC by 13.03% when 𝑘 = 50 . The 
result indicates that the cross-layer message passing mechanism plays

a crucial role in capturing the inter-layer dependencies in multilayer 
heterogeneous networks with heterogeneous topological structures.
To evaluate the effect of cross-layer degree features, SACRL is com­
pared with SACRL-CD. SACRL outperforms SACRL-CD on the Arxiv 
network at k=50, with the notable improvement of 6.36%. On the 
four multilayer heterogeneous networks (BC17, MU78, Cal65, and 
Paris), the performance difference between SACRL and SACRL-CD is 
marginal, indicating that the graph transformer’s cross-layer message 
passing mechanism already captures the inter-layer structural informa­
tion through attention-weighted aggregation over inter-layer neighbors, 
rendering the inter-layer degree feature largely redundant. However, 
on the large-scale Aminer network, which features denser inter-layer 
connectivity, SACRL-CD achieves a higher influence spread due to the 
richer cross-layer structural signals. This suggests that explicitly encod­
ing inter-layer degree is beneficial when inter-layer connections are 
exceptionally dense. Therefore, for the case where the inter-layer edges 
are sparse, SACRL’s implicit cross-layer message passing provides suf­
ficient and generalizable representations. On the contrary, for cases 
where there are dense inter-layer connections, incorporating cross-layer 
degrees as an input feature further enhances the performance of SACRL.
To validate the role of the 63-dimensional constant vector, SACRL 
is compared with SACRL-CV. SACRL outperforms SACRL-CV on most 
networks, with the most improvements of 6.19% on the Cal65 network 
and 1.95% on the MU78 network at 𝑘= 50 .

__data__.text:
SACRL outperforms SACRL-CV on most 
networks, with the most improvements of 6.19% on the Cal65 network 
and 1.95% on the MU78 network at 𝑘= 50 . The magnitude of the per­
formance gap correlates with the total structural complexity of each 
network, measured by the average total edge count per node across both 
intra-layer and inter-layer edges: Cal65 (10.91), BC17 (5.59), MU78 
(4.92), Aminer (4.76), Arxiv (3.59), and Paris (1.22). In networks with 
higher total edge density, node centrality values exhibit a wider distri­
bution, resulting in many structurally peripheral nodes with near-zero 
feature entries. Without the constant vector, the ELU pre-activations 
driven by such sparse inputs fall into the low-gradient regime, im­
peding parameter updates for peripheral nodes. The constant vector 
provides a learnable bias with 63 independent parameter columns, shift­
ing pre-activations into the effective gradient region of ELU and ensuring 
non-zero gradient updates for all nodes regardless of their structural 
centrality. On large-scale networks, the performance difference is neg­
ligible, as the constant vector’s contribution is diluted during inference 
when Q-values are ranked across thousands of nodes, and its primary 
role lies in stabilizing training on small synthetic graphs.
The ablation studies demonstrate that the performance of SACRL is 
enhanced by integrating the structural feature extraction, Dueling DQN 
architecture, and cross-layer message passing mechanisms. The com­
prehensive integration is essential for optimizing influence spread in 
multilayer heterogeneous networks with diverse topological character­
istics.
5.4 . Parameter configurations
The sensitivity of SACRL to the N-step return horizon N was inves­
tigated through parameter experiments. Following the DRL-based work 
for influence maximization [ 27], 𝑘 is fixed at 5, as larger values have 
been shown to cause significant computational training overhead with 
negligible performance gain.

__data__.text:
Following the DRL-based work 
for influence maximization [ 27], 𝑘 is fixed at 5, as larger values have 
been shown to cause significant computational training overhead with 
negligible performance gain. The model is trained for 1000 epochs on 
ER multilayer heterogeneous networks consisting of 50 nodes per layer, 
with N set to 3, 4, and 5, respectively. Each configuration is executed 
five times independently, and the influence spread is evaluated every 
100 epochs on the BA network comprising 500 nodes per layer. The 
results are presented in Fig. 5.
As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the curve with N=4 consistently achieves 
the highest mean influence spread, while the curve with N=3 starts 
at a comparable level and exhibits a slight downward trend in the later 
training stages. Fig. 5(b) further illustrates the distribution of influence 
spread across all evaluation checkpoints, where N=4 shows a greater 
density toward higher values compared to the other two configurations. 
The mean influence spread reaches its maximum at N=4, followed 
by N=3 and N=5, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (c). Moreover, all three 
configurations exhibit the variability across runs, which is expected 
given the combinatorial nature of the MHIM problem. The complex 
inter-layer link patterns in multilayer heterogeneous networks create 
multiple local optima in the solution space. Furthermore, the cascading 
effect across layers amplifies the uncertainty in influence estimation, as 
the activation of a seed node propagates through both intra-layer and 
inter-layer edges, leading to diverse diffusion outcomes. Additionally, 
as the seed set expands, the coverage regions of high-value nodes grad­
ually overlap, resulting in diminishing marginal gains that reduce the 
discriminability of subsequent decisions and increase the difficulty of 
policy learning.
Despite the variability observed during the 1000 training epochs, 
N=4 achieves a distinctly higher mean influence spread, marking the 
configuration as a highly effective trade-off for the MHIM problem. The 
setting N=4 is therefore adopted as the default in all the experiments.

Despite the variability observed during the 1000 training epochs, 
N=4 achieves a distinctly higher mean influence spread, marking the 
configuration as a highly effective trade-off for the MHIM problem. The 
setting N=4 is therefore adopted as the default in all the experiments.
5.5 . Performance comparisons with baselines
5.5.1 . Comparison on influence spread
To demonstrate the effectiveness of SACRL, nine baseline algorithms 
were compared: CIM, CPR, CWIIIF, AGNN, BiGDN, CELF, GEHC, DC, 
and K-shell, in terms of influence spread on six multilayer heterogeneous 
networks under the proposed propagation model. The intra-layer spread 
probability was fixed at 𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑟𝑎 = 0.01, the seed set sizes were varied be­
tween 10 and 50, and the Monte Carlo simulations were performed for 
2000 times. Fig. 6 illustrates the influence spread when 𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑟𝑎 = 0.01.
By analyzing the results in Fig. 6 , it is evident that SACRL out­
performs all algorithms except the CELF in most cases. As shown in 
Fig. 6(a)–(f), the proposed SACRL achieves superior performance in in­
fluence propagation across six multi-layer heterogeneous networks with 
diverse scales and topological structures. In Fig. 6 (a), when 𝑘= 50 , 
SACRL achieves an influence spread closely approaching that of CELF 
while outperforming all other baselines. On the contrary, CWIIIF per­
forms poorly on all multilayer heterogeneous networks. The reason why 
CWIIIF performs the worst on all networks is that CWIIIF selects a unified 
seed set throughout the entire network, without being able to distinguish 
the importance of nodes at different layers in multilayer heterogeneous 
networks.

C. Li, J. Tang, L. Liu et al.
Fig. 6. Comparison on influence spread between ten algorithms on the six multilayer heterogeneous networks (𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑟𝑎 = 0.01).
As shown in Fig. 6 , CELF consistently achieves the highest influ­
ence spread across all six multilayer heterogeneous networks, serving 
as an approximate upper bound owing to its exhaustive greedy evalu­
ation via Monte Carlo simulations. On the five networks (Arxiv, BC17, 
MU78, Cal65, and Paris), the influence spread of SACRL is close to that 
of CELF, demonstrating near-optimal performance. On the large-scale 
Aminer network, CELF outperforms SACRL by 14.34% at 𝑘= 50 . This 
larger gap can be attributed to the scale and dense inter-layer connec­
tivity of Aminer, where the MHIC model produces complex cascading 
dynamics that are difficult to approximate with the proxy reward. In 
contrast, CELF’s direct Monte Carlo evaluation captures global influence 
spread but at a higher computational cost, which becomes prohibitive 
at scale. SACRL bridges this trade-off by learning a generalizable pol­
icy that approximates near-optimal selection with significantly lower 
inference overhead.
On the Cal65 network in Fig. 6 (d), compared with the second-
best algorithm AGNN, SACRL still maintains a 3.58% improvement. In 
Fig. 6(e), SACRL achieves approximately 277 at 𝑘= 50 , outperforming 
AGNN by 2.5% and BiGDN by 2.2%. Although AGNN and BiGDN capture 
intra-layer structural importance in multilayer heterogeneous networks, 
they face limitations in addressing the inter-layer interactions inherent 
to real-world multilayer systems.

__data__.text:
Although AGNN and BiGDN capture 
intra-layer structural importance in multilayer heterogeneous networks, 
they face limitations in addressing the inter-layer interactions inherent 
to real-world multilayer systems. Specifically, since AGNN and BiGDN 
focus on single-layer or aggregated topological feature extraction, they 
fail to fully consider the impact of many-to-many cross-layer connec­
tions on node importance. In such multilayer heterogeneous networks, 
the inter-layer interaction is fundamental to identifying key spreaders. 
The performance gain is more pronounced on networks with richer 
inter-layer connections, such as the Arxiv and Cal65 networks, where 
modeling cross-layer interactions is critical and SACRL captures the in­
tricate cross-layer topological structures more effectively than AGNN or 
BiGDN.
The magnitude of SACRL’s improvement over AGNN and BiGDN 
varies across datasets and is related to inter-layer structural complex­
ity. On the Aminer network, which features a large scale and dense 
inter-layer connections, AGNN’s autoencoder-based architecture can ef­
fectively extract single-layer features and identify high-quality seed 
nodes, which maximizes intra-layer propagation to create more opportu­
nities for cross-layer spread. Similarly, on the Paris network, the minimal 
inter-layer coupling suppresses the effect of cross-layer-aware methods, 
causing all algorithms to converge toward comparable performance.
In contrast to machine learning-based methods, the traditional 
centrality-based algorithms DC and K-shell exhibit weaker performance 
on most networks. At 
𝑘= 50 , DC achieves only 82.36% of SACRL’s in­
fluence spread on the Cal65 network, while K-shell achieves 82.51% on 
the same network. GEHC, which augments degree and K-shell central­
ity with DeepWalk-based graph embeddings, achieves slightly higher 
scores than DC and K-shell on several networks but still falls short of 
SACRL by 17.28% on the Cal65 network and 14.59% on the MU78 net­
work.

__data__.text:
The performance gap reflects the structural characteristics of these 
networks: the Cal65 network features high intra-layer density coupled 
with inter-layer connectivity, creating complex cross-layer propagation 
pathways that simple degree or coreness rankings fail to capture. The 
three methods share a common limitation in that they do not explic­
itly model many-to-many inter-layer connections, leading to seed sets 
that are locally central but globally suboptimal in multilayer heteroge­
neous networks. The exception is the Paris network, where the sparse 
inter-layer connectivity renders the cross-layer topology negligible, al­
lowing local centrality to approximate node importance with reasonable 
accuracy.
On the Paris network, the performance differences among algorithms 
are minimal. This convergence is primarily due to the network’s ex­
tremely sparse inter-layer connectivity, comprising only 64 inter-layer 
edges, which renders cross-layer propagation negligible and can reduce 
the problem to near-independent intra-layer spreading. Under such con­
ditions, the topological structure is sufficiently simple that the most 
critical nodes can be readily identified without sophisticated cross-layer 
modeling, thereby limiting the advantage of the proposed SACRL. To 
further compare the effectiveness, Table 2  presents the comparative 
performance and rankings of all algorithms at 𝑘= 50 . The influence 
spread rankings of the proposed SACRL and baseline algorithms across 
different multilayer heterogeneous networks demonstrate that SACRL 
consistently achieves top-tier performance. 
5.5.2 . Comparison on the seed set
To provide more insight into the performance of the proposed 
SACRL, the distribution of the seed nodes in the multilayer heteroge­
neous network is evaluated by the average distance between seeds. A 
larger value of the metric indicates that the selected seed nodes are 
more widely distributed across the network layers, which can reduce

Fig. 7  shows the average distance between the seeds selected by 
different algorithms across six multilayer heterogeneous networks. The 
experimental results demonstrate that the seeds selected by BiGDN 
and CIM, as well as traditional centrality methods such as DC and 
K-shell, exhibit higher average distances in most networks. In compar­
ison, the average distance among seeds selected by SACRL remains at 
a relatively low level across all six multilayer heterogeneous networks. 
Nevertheless, SACRL achieves superior performance in influence spread. 
This non-linear relationship between seed distance and performance 
suggests that a large spatial dispersion is not a prerequisite for effective 
influence maximization on multilayer heterogeneous networks. While 
a relatively concentrated distribution might imply potential influence 
overlap, SACRL avoids the excessive dispersion. For DC and K-shell, the 
large average distance reflects their tendency to identify hubs within 
individual layers that lack inter-layer connectivity, failing to propagate 
across the multilayer networks.
To further evaluate the spreading capability of seed nodes in mul­
tilayer heterogeneous networks, the external cross-layer edges, which 
represent the inter-layer connections where one endpoint is a seed node 
and the other is a non-seed node, were analyzed in Fig. 8. The metric 
measures the potential of seed nodes to spread influence across differ­
ent layers to unactivated regions.

8. The metric 
measures the potential of seed nodes to spread influence across differ­
ent layers to unactivated regions. Fig. 8(a) and (b) present the external 
cross-layer edges of seed nodes selected by ten algorithms across most 
datasets. This indicates that the seeds selected by SACRL possess su­
perior one-hop cross-layer spreading capability. While BiGDN, DC, and 
K-shell show larger average distances, their cross-layer edges rank lower, 
confirming that wide distribution does not necessarily correlate with 
strong inter-layer connectivity. SACRL’s advantage lies in identifying 
seeds that, despite being more clustered, act as vital cross-layer bridges. 
Notably, CELF exhibits fewer external cross-layer edges than SACRL on 
several networks yet achieves the highest influence spread. This shows 
that external cross-layer edges only measure one-hop direct connections, 
while CELF uses multi-step simulations to find seeds that can activate 
more nodes further downstream.
Based on the results shown in Figs. 7 and 8, SACRL demonstrates 
an effective seed selection strategy by achieving the highest external 
cross-layer connectivity while maintaining a moderate average distance, 
which enables efficient influence propagation across both intra-layer 
and inter-layer pathways. Finally, the findings suggest that effective 
influence maximization in multilayer heterogeneous networks requires 
careful consideration of both intra-layer distribution and inter-layer 
connectivity of seed nodes.
5.6 . Graph transformer analysis
To evaluate the necessity of the GT architecture and the rationality 
of mean pooling, four additional ablation variants are designed: SACRL-
GCN replaces the GT encoder with a Graph Convolutional Network. 
SACRL-MLP eliminates graph-based message passing entirely and ap­
plies an MLP to raw node features. SACRL-MP and SACRL-SP substitute 
max pooling and sum pooling for mean pooling, respectively. The results 
are shown in 
Fig. 9.

SACRL consistently outperforms SACRL-GCN and SACRL-MLP across 
all six networks, confirming that performance gains cannot be attributed 
solely to handcrafted structural features. When 𝑘= 50 , SACRL achieves 
improvement rates over SACRL-GCN of 42.44% on the Cal65 network 
and 37.30% on the Aminer network, and outperforms SACRL-MLP by 
16.52% on Aminer network. Since SACRL and SACRL-GCN share identi­
cal input features, the gap isolates the contribution of the GT’s attention-
weighted aggregation over GCN’s uniform normalization. In the Cal65 
and Aminer networks, GCN’s uniform symmetric normalization aggre­
gates neighbor information without discrimination, which may cause 
over-smoothing of node representations and reduce the discriminability 
of structurally distinct nodes. The GT’s attention mechanism mitigates 
this issue by assigning differentiated weights to neighbors, thereby pre­
serving structural heterogeneity in the learned embeddings. SACRL-MLP 
exhibits the overall performance degradation, confirming that topology-
aware message passing is indispensable and that structural features 
alone are insufficient for effective node representation.
Regarding pooling strategy, SACRL with mean pooling achieves the 
highest influence spread across all six networks, with the performance 
gap most evident in Fig. 9(c), where SACRL outperforms SACRL-MP by 
2.63% and SACRL-SP by 1.83% at 𝑘= 50 .

__data__.text:
9(c), where SACRL outperforms SACRL-MP by 
2.63% and SACRL-SP by 1.83% at 𝑘= 50 . Max pooling retains only 
the maximum activation per embedding dimension, which is dominated 
by structurally extreme nodes in dense networks and produces a bi­
ased global state representation. Sum pooling accumulates embeddings 
across all nodes, causing the graph embedding magnitude to scale with 
network size. This inconsistency between the small training networks 
and large inference networks undermines cross-network generalization. 
Mean pooling normalizes by node count, yielding a scale-invariant 
global state representation that remains stable across networks of vastly 
different sizes. On small networks such as the Paris and BC17 networks, 
all pooling strategies yield comparable results, as the reduced node count 
limits variance between pooling operations.
The results confirm that the GT architecture contributes beyond 
handcrafted feature engineering, and that mean pooling provides the 
most robust and generalizable global state representation for the Dueling 
DQN across multilayer heterogeneous networks of diverse topological 
characteristics.
5.7 . Comparison on time efficiency
To assess the computational efficiency and scalability of SACRL, 
the inference time was evaluated across six multilayer heterogeneous 
networks against CELF as a baseline and against two machine learning-
based methods, AGNN and BiGDN. The inference time is defined as the 
total time required to select seed nodes across all 𝐿 layers for a given 
network at seed budget 𝑘. Furthermore, the resource consumption of 
SACRL was measured as the peak memory usage during the inference 
process. Table 3 shows the training time on synthetic networks.

Fig. 10
 presents the inference time at 𝑘 = 30. CELF incurs the highest 
runtime across all datasets, as its greedy strategy evaluates the marginal 
gain of every candidate node via thousands of Monte Carlo simulations 
at each selection step, resulting in a time cost that grows rapidly with 
both network size and seed budget. AGNN exhibits higher inference 
time than the two DRL-based methods. This is because AGNN adopts 
an autoencoder architecture: when applied to a new target network, the 
autoencoder must be re-executed on the new graph to generate node em­
beddings, precluding the amortization of representation learning across 
different networks. In contrast, BiGDN is a DRL-based method whose 
trained parameters generalize directly to unseen networks, and infer­
ence reduces to a single forward pass. SACRL requires recomputing the 
node embeddings after seed selection. While the overhead is negligible 
on small networks, it incurs additional time on large-scale networks, re­
sulting in lower time efficiency compared to BiGDN. However, SACRL 
significantly outperforms BiGDN on influence spread. 
Table 4 further reports SACRL’s inference time, Monte Carlo eval­
uation time, peak GPU memory, and peak CPU memory for 𝑘 ∈
{10, 20, 30, 40, 50}. The inference time increases approximately linearly 
with 𝑘, as each additional seed selection requires a recomputation of 
embeddings for the remaining candidate nodes. Peak memory remains 
constant across all values of 𝑘 for a given dataset since the graph 
structure is fixed during inference, and scales with network size.

5.8 . Generalization and proxy reward analysis
5.8.1 . Distribution shift and generalization analysis
SACRL was trained on synthetic ER networks (50 nodes per layer) 
but tested on real-world networks with different topological charac­
teristics. To quantify this generalization gap, the topological statistics 
and distributional distances between training and test networks were 
computed.
Table 5 reports the key topological statistics. The training networks 
have low clustering coefficients, while real networks exhibit higher clus­
tering coefficients. The average degree difference is marginal, but the 
skewness gap and clustering gap are significant, confirming that the dis­
tributional shift lies primarily in degree heterogeneity and community 
structure.

6 presents the two-sample Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) test re­
sults. All 𝑝-values are below 0.001, confirming statistically significant 
distributional shifts across all training–test pairs. The Paris network ex­
hibits the largest shift ( 𝐷deg = 0.8481, 𝐷CC = 0.8579) due to its sparse 
topology, while BC17 and MU78 show relatively moderate shifts (𝐷deg <
0.30, 𝐷CC < 0.20). 
Despite the distributional gaps, SACRL ranks second only to CELF 
on all six networks. The generalization stems from three factors: (1) 
the layer-wise feature normalization provides scale-invariant represen­
tations, (2) the attention-weighted cross-layer message passing adapts 
to local topology, and (3) the random layer selection during training 
promotes a layer-agnostic policy.
To examine whether a more realistic graph generator improves gen­
eralization, SACRL-LFR is trained using LFR benchmark networks with 
planted communities. Table 7 presents the results. SACRL outperforms 
SACRL-LFR on five of six datasets across all seed budgets. SACRL-LFR is 
marginally better only on Aminer at 𝑘 ≤ 20, where the LFR community 
structure provides an initial advantage for identifying locally influential 
seeds.

SACRL-LFR is 
marginally better only on Aminer at 𝑘 ≤ 20, where the LFR community 
structure provides an initial advantage for identifying locally influential 
seeds. However, as 𝑘 increases, ER-trained SACRL surpasses SACRL-LFR 
from 𝑘 = 30 onward. This result suggests that the structural simplic­
ity of ER networks forces the agent to rely on learned features rather 
than memorizing specific community patterns, thereby yielding more 
generalizable representations.
5.8.2 . Proxy reward validation
To validate the accuracy of the proposed proxy reward function, cor­
relation analysis is conducted between the proxy estimates and the true 
influence spread measured by Monte Carlo simulation. For each test net­
work and layer, 50 random seed sets of size 𝑘 = 5  are generated, and 
both the proxy estimate Φ(S) and the true MC influence 𝜎(S) with 1000 
simulation runs are computed. The Spearman rank correlation ( 𝜌) and 
Pearson linear correlation ( 𝑟) are reported. Fig. 11(a) presents the cor­
relation coefficients across synthetic networks of varying sizes and the 
BC17 network, and Fig. 11(b) visualizes the linear relationship between 
proxy and MC estimates on the BC17 network.
All correlations are statistically significant ( 𝑝 < 0.01), confirming 
that the proxy reward consistently preserves the relative ranking of 
seed set quality. A notable finding is that the correlation coefficients 
are substantially higher on the real-world BC17 network compared to 
the small synthetic networks. The proxy reward formula is designed 
based on structure: the LIE estimator captures intra-layer two-hop acti­
vation potential, while the cross-layer component estimates inter-layer 
influence contribution weighted by connectivity density. These struc­
tural approximations align closely with the actual diffusion dynamics, 
yielding ranking agreement.
The marginal gain ranking accuracy is evaluated by computing the 
proxy and MC marginal gains for individual nodes selected as the sole 
seed.

These struc­

yielding ranking agreement.
The marginal gain ranking accuracy is evaluated by computing the 
proxy and MC marginal gains for individual nodes selected as the sole 
seed. On a synthetic network with 40 nodes per layer, the Spearman cor­
relation between proxy and MC marginal gains is 0.502, and the Top-5 
overlap rate (i.e., the fraction of the proxy’s top-5 ranked nodes that also 
appear in the MC’s top-5) is 40%. For reinforcement learning, the criti­
cal requirement is that the reward signal preserves the ordinal ranking 
of actions, not that it provides exact influence estimates. The consis­
tently significant correlations confirm that the proxy reward satisfies 
this requirement.

__data__.text:
C. Li, J. Tang, L. Liu et al.
Fig. 12. True influence spread (MC simulation, 2000 runs) of seed sets selected 
by proxy-trained greedy, degree centrality, and random strategies on a synthetic 
3-layer network (60 nodes per layer, 𝑘= 5 ). The green annotations indicate the 
improvement of proxy-greedy over random selection.
To validate that the proxy reward function itself provides an effec­
tive optimization signal, we compare seed sets selected by maximizing 
the proxy marginal gain against degree centrality and random selec­
tion. All influence values are measured by true MC simulation. Fig. 12 
presents the results on a synthetic 3-layer network with 60 nodes per 
layer and seed budget 
𝑘= 5 . The proxy-greedy strategy consistently 
outperforms the baselines, confirming that the proxy reward correctly 
identifies high-quality seeds. Building upon this validated reward func­
tion, the full SACRL framework employs Dueling DQN to learn a policy 
that generalizes across networks. 
The greedy strategy guided by the proxy marginal gain consistently 
achieves the highest true MC influence across all three layers, outper­
forming random selection by 2.68%–5.12% and degree centrality by up 
to 3.55%. The improvement over degree centrality, which only considers 
intra-layer connectivity, highlights that the proxy reward’s cross-layer 
component successfully identifies seeds with superior cross-layer propa­
gation capability. Although these improvements are observed on a small 
synthetic network (60 nodes per layer), the performance advantages 
are more pronounced on real-world networks. The consistent superior­
ity of the SACRL over all baselines under true MC evaluation across all 
six real-world networks provides the strongest evidence that optimizing 
the validated proxy reward leads to policies that maximize the correct 
objective.

5.9 . Statistical results
To ensure the statistical validity of the experimental results, the 
Wilcoxon signed-rank test was performed. The Wilcoxon signed-rank 
test is a non-parametric statistical method suitable for assessing whether 
significant differences exist between two comparative algorithms. The 
significance level was set at 
𝛼= 0.05 . The results across the six multilayer 
heterogeneous networks are summarized in Table 8, where the 𝑍-value 
measures the magnitude of the performance difference, and the 𝑝-value 
indicates the probability of observing these differences under the null 
hypothesis. A 
𝑝-value below 0.05 confirms a statistically significant dif­
ference. Additionally, 𝑁+ represents the number of networks where the 
proposed SACRL algorithm outperforms the baseline algorithm, while 
𝑁− indicates the instances where SACRL underperforms.
According to the results in Table 8, SACRL demonstrates statistically 
significant superiority over CIM, CPR, CWIIIF, BiGDN, and GEHC across 
all evaluated seed budgets. When compared with traditional centrality 
methods such as DC and K-shell, SACRL maintains a consistent statistical 
advantage for all 
𝑘 values. In the comparison with AGNN, no significant 
differences are observed at budgets ranging from 𝑘= 10  to 𝑘= 40 . 
However, SACRL exhibits statistically significant superiority at 𝑘= 50 . 
Conversely, CELF achieves higher influence spread than SACRL, showing

cal analysis validates the efficacy of the SACRL compared to the majority 
of the baseline methods.
6 . Discussion
The effectiveness of the SACRL algorithm relies on its ability to model 
the complex topology in multilayer heterogeneous networks. The abla­
tion studies demonstrate that the performance of SACRL is enhanced 
by integrating the structural feature extraction, Dueling DQN archi­
tecture, and cross-layer message passing mechanisms. This integration 
enables SACRL to capture complex intra-layer and inter-layer topologi­
cal information in an effective way.

__data__.text:

the high cost of Monte Carlo simulations. While the efficient estimator 
is anchored in second-order activation potential and effectively reduces 
training time, it inherently focuses the agent on local topological satu­
ration. Consequently, a marginal approximation gap may exist between 
the neighborhood-based approximation and the true global influence 
spread. To bridge the gap, a promising direction for future research is 
to explore hybrid reward mechanisms that incorporate periodic global 
feedback, thereby aligning the learned policy more closely with global 
optimality while preserving training efficiency.
Furthermore, the current propagation model relies on inter-layer 
connectivity density to estimate diffusion probabilities and, within a 
given layer pair (𝑖, 𝑗), assigns a uniform activation probability 𝑞𝑖𝑗 to all 
inter-layer edges, thereby abstracting away node-pair-specific connec­
tion strengths. While this static formulation is effective for structural 
analysis, it does not fully capture the dynamic nature of real-world 
propagation, which is often modulated by temporal shifts and multi-
dimensional user attributes. Future research should transcend these 
static constraints by incorporating fine-grained transmission probabili­
ties informed by interaction frequency or edge weights (when available), 
as well as dynamic node features, and by extending the approach to 
multilayer higher-order networks. In addition, extending the current 
per-layer budget to a global budget with adaptive layer-wise allocation 
offers another promising direction, as it enables optimal resource dis­
tribution across layers under a fixed total budget while respecting the 
varying influence potential of each layer.
The feasibility of identified seeds under structural damages is also 
a vital consideration for practical deployment. The SACRL does not 
account for robustness issues in multilayer heterogeneous networks. 
Given the correlation between network robustness and influence max­
imization [52], the integration of structural resilience into the SACRL 
framework represents a promising future research direction.

__data__.text:
The SACRL does not 
account for robustness issues in multilayer heterogeneous networks. 
Given the correlation between network robustness and influence max­
imization [52], the integration of structural resilience into the SACRL 
framework represents a promising future research direction. By incorpo­
rating robustness-aware metrics into the reward function, agents could 
be trained to prioritize seeds that maintain propagation efficacy despite 
topological perturbations, thereby ensuring more stable performance in 
disturbed environments. Enhancing these aspects will further elevate the 
algorithm’s practical value in real-world scenarios, such as managing 
cross-platform public opinion to mitigate echo chambers or designing 
propagation-aware containment strategies for epidemic control.
7 . Conclusion
In this paper, we propose a structure-aware cross-layer node rep­
resentation algorithm to identify the optimal seed set for influence 
maximization in multilayer heterogeneous networks. A multilayer het­
erogeneous network independent cascade model is developed to char­
acterize the intra-layer and inter-layer diffusion dynamics based on 
inter-layer connectivity density. Furthermore, a proxy reward function 
based on an expected influence estimator is designed. The proposed 
SACRL learns node representations through a graph transformer-based 
GNN encoder and employs a Dueling DQN to optimize the decision-
making policy for seed selection. The performance of the proposed 
SACRL was evaluated on six real-world multilayer heterogeneous net­
works. Subsequent research will be dedicated to scaling the proposed 
SACRL to large-scale multilayer higher-order networks and applying 
the SACRL to address practical problems such as cross-platform public 
opinion control and epidemic spreading.

__data__.text:
# ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

## Abstract
We present ReAct, a paradigm that combines reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner for large language models. By generating both verbal reasoning traces (e.g., "I need to search for X") and actions (e.g., calling a search API), ReAct enables models to dynamically reason about what information is needed, act to acquire it, and refine subsequent reasoning based on observations. This approach addresses key limitations of pure chain-of-thought reasoning, particularly in handling incomplete information and adapting to external feedback.

## Key Method
ReAct alternates between two modes: (1) **Thought**: generating natural language reasoning about the current state and next steps, (2) **Act**: invoking external tools such as search or calculation APIs. The model observes the tool output and continues the thought-action loop until a final answer is reached.

## Experimental Results
On HotpotQA, ReAct achieves an EM score of 34.6 and F1 of 58.9, outperforming standard prompting (20.4/33.7) and chain-of-thought (26.4/48.7). On Fever, ReAct achieves 62.5% accuracy vs CoT's 55.4%.

## Conclusion
ReAct demonstrates that explicit reasoning traces combined with tool use improves both interpretability and correctness in knowledge-intensive tasks.

__data__.text:
# Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

## Abstract
Reflexion introduces a novel framework that converts environmental feedback into linguistic summary memories, enabling agents to learn from past mistakes without expensive gradient updates. The agent stores successful and failed trajectories as natural language "reflections" in episodic memory, and uses these reflections to guide future decisions.

## Key Method
The Reflexion architecture consists of three components: (1) **Actor**: a policy that generates actions based on current state and memory, (2) **Evaluator**: a function that scores the Actor's trajectory, (3) **Memory**: a persistent store of reflections (natural language summaries of past successes and failures). When the Actor encounters a similar situation, relevant reflections are retrieved from memory and injected into the prompt.

## Experimental Results
On the AlfWorld benchmark, Reflexion improves the success rate from 58% to 80% compared to the baseline ReAct agent. On HotpotQA, it achieves 61% accuracy vs ReAct's 47%. The key insight is that while ReAct learns only within a single episode, Reflexion transfers knowledge across episodes via linguistic memory.

## Conclusion
Verbal reinforcement learning through reflection enables more sample-efficient learning in interactive environments by leveraging language as a compressed representation of experience.

__data__.text:
# RETRO: Improving Language Models by Retrieving from Trillions of Tokens

## Abstract
RETRO (Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer) augments auto-regressive language models with a retrieval mechanism that accesses a massive database of trillions of tokens. Unlike standard language models that rely solely on parametric knowledge, RETRO retrieves relevant text chunks at each generation step and conditions predictions on both the context and retrieved neighbors.

## Key Method
RETRO uses a chunked cross-attention mechanism: (1) the input text is divided into fixed-size chunks, (2) for each chunk, the model retrieves the k nearest neighbors from a database indexed by a frozen BERT-style encoder, (3) cross-attention layers attend to both the retrieved neighbors and the standard decoder context. The retrieval database contains 2 trillion tokens from MassiveText.

## Experimental Results
RETRO achieves GPT-3-level perplexity with 4x fewer parameters (7.5B vs 175B). On question answering, it outperforms similarly sized GPT models by 10-15%. The retrieval mechanism provides a direct path for updating knowledge without retraining.

## Key Insight
Explicit retrieval augments parametric knowledge with dynamic access to external information, making models more parameter-efficient and easier to update. The chunked cross-attention design ensures that retrieval influences generation at every step.

## Limitations
- Requires large pre-built retrieval database
- Frozen encoder cannot adapt to query distribution shifts
- Retrieval latency adds to inference time

__data__.text:
# Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools

## Abstract
Toolformer introduces a self-supervised approach for language models to learn tool use through API calls. The model is trained to decide when and how to call external tools (calculator, search engine, translation system, calendar) by augmenting the training data with API call annotations.

## Key Method
The training process consists of: (1) **Sampling**: for each position in a text, the model samples potential API calls using few-shot examples, (2) **Filtering**: calls that reduce the loss on subsequent tokens are retained, (3) **Fine-tuning**: the original LM is fine-tuned on the augmented corpus containing API calls and their responses. This requires no human annotation or reinforcement learning.

## Experimental Results
Toolformer significantly improves zero-shot performance on tasks requiring tool use: QA (+23%), math (+35%), and temporal reasoning (+18%). The model learns to use each tool appropriately without explicit supervision of when to call which tool.

## Limitations
- Toolformer uses frozen API calls without execution feedback loops
- Cannot adapt tool use based on partial results
- Limited to atomic tool calls rather than multi-step tool chains

## Impact
Toolformer demonstrated that language models can autonomously learn tool use through self-supervision, paving the way for tool-augmented language models.

__data__.text:
Graph Bayesian Optimization for Multiplex Influence Maximization
Zirui Yuan1, Minglai Shao1*, Zhiqian Chen2
1School of New Media and Communication, Tianjin University, China
2Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Mississippi State University, USA
yzr@tju.edu.cn, shaoml@tju.edu.cn, zchen@cse.msstate.edu
Abstract
Influence maximization (IM) is the problem of identifying a
limited number of initial influential users within a social net-
work to maximize the number of influenced users. However,
previous research has mostly focused on individual informa-
tion propagation, neglecting the simultaneous and interactive
dissemination of multiple information items. In reality, when
users encounter a piece of information, such as a smartphone
product, they often associate it with related products in their
minds, such as earphones or computers from the same brand.
Additionally, information platforms frequently recommend
related content to users, amplifying this cascading effect and
leading to multiplex influence diffusion.
This paper first formulates the Multiplex Influence Maxi-
mization (Multi-IM) problem using multiplex diffusion mod-
els with an information association mechanism. In this prob-
lem, the seed set is a combination of influential users and
information. To effectively manage the combinatorial com-
plexity, we propose Graph Bayesian Optimization for Multi-
IM (GBIM). The multiplex diffusion process is thoroughly
investigated using a highly effective global kernelized atten-
tion message-passing module. This module, in conjunction
with Bayesian linear regression (BLR), produces a scalable
surrogate model. A data acquisition module incorporating
the exploration-exploitation trade-off is developed to opti-
mize the seed set further. Extensive experiments on synthetic
and real-world datasets have proven our proposed framework
effective. The code is available at https://github.com/zirui-
yuan/GBIM.
Introduction
The rapid growth of online social networks has sparked sub-
stantial interest among researchers in understanding the dy-
namics of information dissemination within these networks.

This interest has led to the study of influence maximization
(IM), an optimization problem that aims to maximize the
influence spread within a specific diffusion model by select-
ing a limited number of seeds (Kempe, Kleinberg, and Tar-
dos 2003). IM has garnered considerable attention from both
industry and academia due to its relevance in various real-
world applications, including viral marketing, epidemic con-

__data__.text:
Researchers have made great progress in de-
vising various efficient and effective methods to address the
canonical IM problem, utilizing techniques such as simula-
tion, proxy, and sketch (Goyal, Lu, and Lakshmanan 2011;
Borgs et al. 2014; Tang, Shi, and Xiao 2015). In recent
years, IM research has witnessed a growing interest in ex-
ploring more realistic scenarios beyond the canonical set-
ting, including Heterogeneous IM and Multi-Layer IM. Het-
erogeneous IM (Figure 1b) addresses the IM problem in
heterogeneous networks with diverse node and edge types.
Researchers commonly employ techniques involving net-
work schema and meta-paths to construct propagation path-
ways. Building on this, both traditional and learning-based
methods can be designed to optimize the seed set (Zhan
et al. 2015; Deng et al. 2019; Li et al. 2022b). Multi-Layer
IM (Figure 1c) discusses the cross-layer nature of informa-
tion dissemination across different social network platforms.
These studies consider the sharing of nodes between net-
works as a mechanism enabling information to propagate

__data__.text:
across layers. Some approaches for tackling this problem
involve integrating networks into one unified large network
and applying existing IM algorithms (Kuhnle et al. 2018;
Keikha et al. 2020; Katukuri, Jagarapu et al. 2022).
While these efforts have succeeded, they still concen-
trate on scenarios where individual information spreads in
isolation. However, real-world online social networks typ-
ically involve the simultaneous dissemination of multiple
information items, with heterogeneous diffusion patterns
over these items. Additionally, interconnected information
items often result in multiplex influence, further compli-
cating the dynamics of information spread. This study in-
troduces the Multiplex Influence Maximization (Multi-IM)
problem, which aims to maximize the multiplex influence
within the constraints of a fixed seed budget. As depicted
in Figure 1d, three interconnected information items rep-
resented by {v1, v2, v3} (e.g., three complementary prod-
ucts) propagate concurrently in a social network. This can
be modeled as a multiplex network consisting of three prop-
agation layers, each representing the dissemination of one
information item, denoted by the colors red, purple, and
green, respectively. When information item v1 influences
user u1, it creates multiplex influence due to the associa-
tions among the three information items. Consequently, user
u1 also adopts the information from v2 and v3, activating
u1 on the propagation layers of v2 and v3 in this multiplex
network. Moreover, the preference biases of users towards
items lead to different propagation results.
The new mode of propagation presents the following diffi-
culties for seed set selection. First, the presence of multiple
information items increases combinatorial complexity. To
maximize multiplex influence, we must not only select users
to disseminate information but also determine the most per-
tinent information items for those users. Second, this novel
modeling of propagation results in a heterogeneous, multidi-
mensional structure that introduces a new type of geometric
complexity.

__data__.text:
Second, this novel
modeling of propagation results in a heterogeneous, multidi-
mensional structure that introduces a new type of geometric
complexity. Furthermore, compared to Canonical IM, this
propagation modeling generates larger dimensions and data
volume, resulting in catastrophic data processing issues. Nu-
merous layers representing item-specific propagation envi-
ronments would considerably increase computational costs
in Multi-IM, making it challenging for conventional tech-
niques to complete tasks in a timely manner.
We propose GBIM, a Graph Bayesian Optimization
framework for Multi-IM, to tackle these challenges. By ef-
fectively navigating the complex search space, GBIM iden-
tifies the most influential users and the optimal information
items to maximize multiplex influence. To address the het-
erogeneous and multi-layered geometric structure generated
by the new propagation modeling, a highly effective global
kernelized attention message-passing module based on Pos-
itive Random Features (PRF) (Choromanski et al. 2020) is
employed. This module acquires a comprehensive under-
standing of the multiplex diffusion process, yielding a re-
liable influence estimation. GBIM combines this module
with Bayesian linear regression to create a scalable surrogate
model that facilitates efficient data processing. In addition,
the data acquisition module incorporates the exploration-
exploitation trade-off, enabling effective exploration of the
vast search space for optimizing the seed set with the high-
est observed performance. Our contributions are as follows:
• First formulate the Multi-IM problem, which models the
scenario that multiple information items propagate and
interact in a multiplex network.
• Devise a highly effective global kernelized attention
message-passing module to learn the complex multiplex
diffusion process.
• Present GBIM to optimize the seed set with the highest
observed performance efficiently.
• Conduct extensive experiments to demonstrate the per-
formance of the proposed method.

__data__.text:
Related Work
Influence Maximization. The influence maximization
(IM) problem was introduced in seminal work by (Kempe,
Kleinberg, and Tardos 2003), relying on diffusion models
like Linear Threshold and Independent Cascade (Li et al.
2018, 2022a). Much research has developed traditional sim-
ulation, proxy, and sketch-based methods (Goyal, Lu, and
Lakshmanan 2011; Borgs et al. 2014; Tang, Shi, and Xiao
2015), as well as recent learning-based techniques (Ling
et al. 2023). However, these canonical IM studies focus on
single information spread in homogeneous networks. Recent
interest has emerged in more realistic settings like Hetero-
geneous IM and Multi-Layer IM. Heterogeneous IM han-
dles diverse nodes and edges using techniques involving
meta-paths (Zhan et al. 2015; Deng et al. 2019; Li et al.
2022b). Multi-Layer IM examines cross-platform informa-
tion spread via node sharing (Kuhnle et al. 2018; Keikha
et al. 2020; Katukuri, Jagarapu et al. 2022). While incor-
porating complex networks, these works still consider indi-
vidual information. Multi-information settings are first dis-
cussed in Competitive IM, which handles a purely competi-
tive scenario (Bharathi, Kempe, and Salek 2007). The com-
plement relation between information items is only investi-
gated in (Lu, Chen, and Lakshmanan 2015) but is limited to
the IC model. Recently, some studies have examined multi-
information scenarios but did not account for interconnec-
tions between information items (Ni et al. 2020; Wu et al.
2021; Fang, Ho, and Dai 2022). In summary, modeling com-
plex interactions among multiple information items remains
a key limitation. Our work introduces a multiplex network
perspective to model multi-information dissemination.
Bayesian Optimization.

2021; Fang, Ho, and Dai 2022). In summary, modeling com-
plex interactions among multiple information items remains
a key limitation. Our work introduces a multiplex network
perspective to model multi-information dissemination.
Bayesian Optimization. Bayesian optimization (BO)
(Mockus 1998) is a method to optimize complex black-box
functions relying on Bayesian statistical models paired with
optimization algorithms. It requires a surrogate model repre-
senting the target function, typically based on Gaussian pro-
cesses (GPs) (Brochu, Brochu, and De Freitas 2010; Snoek,
Larochelle, and Adams 2012). However, GPs scale cubically
with increasing observations, challenging massively parallel
optimization requiring many evaluations. Neural networks
can serve as a practical surrogate model, leveraging flexi-
ble representation power to model complex functions and
enhance scalability (Snoek et al. 2015; Springenberg et al.
2016; Perrone et al. 2018; Ma, Cui, and Yang 2019). In
The Thirty-Eighth AAAI Conference on Artiﬁcial Intelligence (AAAI-24)

__data__.text:
this work, we develop a highly effective global kernelized
attention message-passing module to learn the multiplex
diffusion process and serve as non-linear basis functions
for Bayesian linear regression to yield a scalable surrogate
model, leveraging both the nonlinear fitting capabilities of
neural networks and the effective statistical properties of
Bayesian.
Problem and Model Definition
In this work, we examine a context where m distinct
pieces of information are concurrently disseminated across
a weighted, directed social network, represented as G =
(U , EU ). Here, U = {u1, u2, ..., un} signifies the user set,
while EU denotes the edge set, with each ei,j ∈ E U carrying
a specific weight wi,j. We introduce the information associ-
ation network as I = (V , EV ), where V = {v1, v2, ..., vm}
represents the set of information items and Ev encompasses
the edges linking these items. Furthermore, we consider a
preference matrix, P ∈ Rn×m, in which the element pi,j re-
flects the inclination of user ui towards item vj. The multi-
plex diffusion modelM is expressed asy = M(x; G, I, P).
In this representation, the input x = {..., (u, v), ...} indi-
cates a seed set containing multiple user-item pairs, while
each pair (u, v) ∈ x implies that useru is initially influenced
by the information item v during the execution of M. The
output y ∈ N+ signifies the multiplex influence exerted by
all items. Grounded on the formalization above, the Multi-
IM problem is defined as follows:
Definition 1 (Multiplex Influence Maximization). The ob-
jective of the Multi-IM problem is to strategically choose a
maximum of k user-item pair from U and V, each user and
item can only be selected once, so as to maximize the overall
multiplex influence.

In reality, the propagation dynamics of each informa-
tion item are different since users are inclined to spread
information aligning with their interests while overlooking
content that does not appeal to their preferences. Besides,
when users are influenced, they may associate other inter-
connected information in their minds, resulting in multiplex
influence. To model this realistic scenario, we introduce the
multiplex influence diffusion modelM along with the asso-
ciation mechanism in a multiplex network perspective.
A multiplex network is a graph with multiple layers,
where each layer contains a network on the same set of
nodes. The multiplex network in M(x; G, I, P) contains m
layers, where each layer models the propagation environ-
ment for one information item and shares the same structure
as G. The commonly used information diffusion models like
Linear Threshold (LT) and Independent Cascade (IC) can
simulate propagation on each layer but need adaption to in-
corporate inherent heterogeneity stemming from preference
matrix P. Specifically, within the propagation layer of in-
formation item vk, the threshold for user ui is adjusted to
1 − pi,k when using the LT model. For the IC model, the in-
fluence probability of edge ei,j is modified to wi,j · pj,k. We
propose an association mechanism to model the inter-layer
propagation across information items:
Definition 2 (Association Mechanism). The Association
Mechanism in the multiplex influence diffusion model refers
to the process by which activated users spread across the
interconnected information item propagation layers.

__data__.text:
We
propose an association mechanism to model the inter-layer
propagation across information items:
Definition 2 (Association Mechanism). The Association
Mechanism in the multiplex influence diffusion model refers
to the process by which activated users spread across the
interconnected information item propagation layers.
Specifically, when user ui gets activated by information
vj, they have a probabilityβ ∗pi,k of associating to other ad-
jacent information vk ∈ N I (vj) and self-activating in vk’s
propagation layer. Here, NI (vj) denotes the neighbor infor-
mation items of vj in I, and β is a base ratio scaling the
inter-layer association strength. This process resembles dif-
fused thinking along I, terminating when user ui makes no
more associations.
Building on the multiplex network structure and associa-
tion mechanism, we formally define the multiplex influence
model as follows:
Definition 3 (Multiplex Diffusion Model). The multiplex
diffusion model M(x; G, I, P) is a model characterizing the
dynamical diffusion process of multiple information items in
a multiplex network. Each layer has a heterogeneous dif-
fusion pattern depending on the preference matrix P, and
the association mechanism allows influenced users to prop-
agate across layers based on I. The output is the expected
activated number at the multiplex network.
Let σi(x) denote the number of the users influenced by
item vi, we define the multiplex influence output by M as:
M(x; G, I, P) =
mX
i=1
σi(x). (2)
The multiplex diffusion model M(x) operates in discrete
time steps. At step 0, the starting state is configured based on
the input seed set x. In each following step, the association
mechanism is executed for every newly activated user. Het-
erogeneous information diffusion then takes place on prop-
agation layers with active users. The model repeats this pro-
cess until no further activations occur and outputs the final
multiplex influence.

GBIM: Graph Bayesian Optimization
Framework for Multi-IM
Framework Overview
The process of our suggested GBIM framework is illus-
trated in Figure 2. This framework encompasses two pri-
mary modules: the surrogate model and data acquisition.
Initially, seeds are generated at random and assessed using
the objective multiplex diffusion model M(x), forming the
preliminary training dataset D = {(x1, y1), ..., (xN , yN )}.
Subsequently, we iteratively: (1) train a surrogate diffusion
model, denoted as M∗(x), to fit the present dataset D and
employ the output from the final layer of M∗(x) as a set
of basis functions ϕ(x), integrate it with a Bayesian linear

Figure 2: The overview of the proposed GBIM framework. This framework includes two modules: surrogate model and data
acquisition. Initially, random seeds are evaluated by the true model M(x) to form dataset D. We then iteratively: (1) train
surrogate model M∗(x) on D; (2) sampling and evaluate the candidates X via M∗(x), selecting top K seed sets to be X ∗; (3)
assess X ∗ via M(x) to expand D. Finally, the optimal x∗ from D with maximal influence is selected.
regressor (BLR) to capture uncertainty; (2) sample an unob-
served candidate set X with the explore-exploit tradeoff, se-
lect the top K seed sets with elevated acquisition values into
X ∗; (3) appraise X ∗ using M(x) to curate a new dataset,
subsequently expanding the training data D. Upon the con-
clusion of the optimization cycle, the optimal x∗ from the
final observed dataset D, showcasing the maximum spread
number, is chosen as the definitive seed set.
Surrogate Model
Message-passing graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely
recognized for modeling geometric structures. By iteratively
updating node representations through aggregating informa-
tion from adjacent nodes, they bear resemblances to the
influence diffusion model. However, prevalent GNNs en-
counter challenges in addressing long-range dependencies,
which are pivotal in the influence diffusion model. In our
study, we introduce an optimized global message-passing
graph neural network to learn the multiplex influence dif-
fusion and leverage a BLR head to capture uncertainty.
Global Kernelized Attention Message Passing.

with a linear kernel.

Data Acquisition
Data acquisition is a critical component in Bayesian Opti-
mization. In this component, we need to trade off explo-
ration and exploitation and quantify the promise of unob-
served inputs using an acquisition function.
Acquisition Function. In this work, we adopt the ex-
pected improvement (EI) as the acquisition function.

__data__.text:
In this component, we need to trade off explo-
ration and exploitation and quantify the promise of unob-
served inputs using an acquisition function.
Acquisition Function. In this work, we adopt the ex-
pected improvement (EI) as the acquisition function. The EI
is defined as the expectation of the improvement function
I(x) = max{0, (µ(x; D) − max(y))} at candidate point x.
It can be formulated as:
aEI (x; D) = σ(x; D)[γ(x)C(γ(x)) + N (γ(x); 0, 1)],
where
γ(x) = µ(x; D) − max(y)
σ(x; D) .
Here C(·) and N (·; 0, 1) denote the cumulative distribution
function and probability density function of the standard
normal distribution, respectively.
Explore-Exploit Tradeoff. At each optimization round,
we sample candidate sets X and compute their acquisition
values. As users and items frequently present in influential
seed sets are more likely to appear in the optimal set, X is
sampled as follows: α from top 5% high influence entries,
the rest uniform randomly, whereα is the exploit ratio. Can-
didates with top 1% acquisition values are chosen as X ∗ to
evaluate via the objective multiplex diffusion model, obtain-
ing new observations D∗ to expand the current dataset D.
Experiments
In the following experiments, we evaluate the effectiveness
of our proposed GBIM framework on four real-world net-
works and one synthetic network for maximizing multiplex
influence across a range of seed set sizes.
Experiment Setup
We evaluate the expected multiplex influence defined in
Equation 2 under Multi-LT and Multi-IC, two multiplex dif-
fusion models extended by LT and IC. We enable each mul-
tiplex diffusion model to simulate until the diffusion process
stops and report the average multiplex influence over 100
simulations.
Datasets. We evaluate GBIM against other methods on
four real-world social networking datasets: Ciao, Epinions1,
Delicious and LastFM 2.

four real-world social networking datasets: Ciao, Epinions1,
Delicious and LastFM 2. These datasets containing genuine
user-item interactions (i.e., ratings or frequencies) are instru-
mental in constructing a pragmatic preference matrix for the
multiplex diffusion model. We also use a synthetic Erdos-
Renyi (Erd˝os, R´enyi et al. 1960) random graph with 30,000
nodes. For each real dataset, we sample some items and con-
struct the item-item association network I by calculating
cosine similarity over user interaction records (item pairs
with similarity above 0.5 are connected). We use item-based
collaborative filtering to generate the user preference matrix
